planting.note: Sow fresh seeds immediately after harvest in fall directly into waterlogged mud or still-shallow water (6–24 inches deep) at pond margins, slow rivers, or lake edges; seeds must never be allowed to dry out — store in water if not planting at once.

directions: Sow fresh, never-dried seeds in fall directly into waterlogged mud or 6–24 inches of still water at the margins of ponds, lakes, or slow-moving rivers; wild rice is one of the most ecologically and culturally important native grains of North America, forming dense stands in the Great Lakes region that have sustained Ojibwe and other Indigenous peoples for millennia and providing critical food for migrating waterfowl. The tall, feathery annual grass germinates in spring when water warms, forms a floating seedling stage, then rockets upward to 4–10 feet by midsummer, bearing separate male and female florets on a large open panicle. Grain shatters and falls into the water when ripe in late summer — harvest by canoe, bending stalks over the gunwale and knocking seeds loose. Because viability is lost within days of drying, only purchase or plant seeds stored in water.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring and plant in consistently wet or saturated soil at pond margins or rain gardens; tussock sedge is an easy-going, highly adaptable native that establishes quickly in wet conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently wet, boggy, or seasonally flooded soil in full sun to partial shade at the margins of ponds, rain gardens, wet meadows, or restored marshes; tussock sedge is one of the most structurally distinctive native sedges of eastern North America, forming large elevated clumps or pedestals (tussocks) that rise above the waterline as years of fibrous root and stem debris accumulate beneath the living foliage. These tussocks serve as critical nesting platforms for marsh wrens, song sparrows, and other wetland birds, and as stepping stones for small mammals moving through flooded habitats. The dark green, arching foliage reaches 18–36 inches and produces narrow brown seed spikes in early summer that provide additional food for waterfowl. Once established, plants are essentially self-sufficient and spread slowly to fill wet margins without becoming aggressive.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring and plant in wet to seasonally flooded soil with partial shade; hop sedge thrives in the dappled light and saturated soils of bottomland woods and stream edges.

directions: Plant in wet to seasonally flooded soil in partial to full shade at the margins of woodland streams, pond edges beneath tree canopy, or shaded rain gardens; hop sedge is a bold native sedge instantly recognized by its spectacular inflated seed heads — large, cylindrical, bright green perigynia packed tightly on upright spikes that closely resemble the dried cones of the hop vine, making it one of the most ornamentally distinctive native sedges in cultivation. It is particularly valuable for shaded wetland restoration projects where many other native sedges struggle, tolerating heavier shade than Carex stricta while still requiring reliably wet soil. The broad, bright-green strap-like leaves reach 18–30 inches and remain attractive through summer; seed heads mature to straw-tan by fall and persist through winter, providing structure and food for birds. Excellent companion to skunk cabbage, sensitive fern, and lizard's tail in shaded bog gardens.

planting.note: Plant softwood or semi-hardwood cuttings or container-grown shrubs in spring in moist to wet, organically rich soil at streambanks, pond edges, or low-lying areas prone to seasonal flooding.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade along streambanks, pond margins, and low-lying areas subject to periodic flooding; false indigo bush is a fast-growing, wide-ranging native shrub found from Ontario to Florida and west to the Great Plains, producing striking 3–6 inch racemes of deep purple flowers with bright orange anthers that create a vivid two-tone contrast in late spring — one of the most visually dramatic native shrubs in flower. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it actively improves soil fertility and is an excellent anchor species for riparian restoration and streambank erosion control projects. The fine-textured, pinnately compound foliage remains attractive through the season, and the plant tolerates brief inundation that would kill most shrubs. Bees — especially bumble bees and native leafcutter bees — heavily visit the flowers. In rich, moist conditions it can sucker to form large colonies, which is desirable for mass plantings and erosion control; remove suckers to keep it shrub-sized in garden settings.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seeds in fall for natural cold-moist stratification, or sow in spring after 30–60 days of cold stratification; plant in well-drained, dry to mesic soil in partial shade at the edge of open woodlands.

directions: Sow scarified seeds in fall or stratified seeds in early spring in dry to moderately moist, well-drained soil in partial shade at the edges of open deciduous woods, woodland clearings, or dry savannas; violet bush clover is a slender, graceful native legume of the eastern United States, producing a profusion of small, bright violet pea-shaped flowers in loose panicles in mid to late summer — a season when few other native perennials are in bloom, making it a valuable late-season nectar source for native bees, skippers, and sulphur butterflies. Unlike the weedy Asian lespedezas that have invaded roadsides across the East, this species is well-behaved and does not spread aggressively. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it enriches the soil and performs best in the lean, dry, slightly acidic soils that characterize the open woodlands and dry savannas of its native range; rich, moist soils cause floppy, rank growth. The trifoliate leaves are small and attractive; stems are reddish and arching. An excellent understory addition to dry native plant gardens and oak savanna restorations.
